core_intuitions: Reasoning is a search problem: the correct answer lies at the END of a PATH through a reasoning tree, not at the top of the next-token distribution — more test-time compute buys a wider/deeper search of that tree.

Chain-of-thought works by externalising intermediate state into the context window: it turns a fixed-depth single forward pass into a variable-depth sequential computation, because each emitted token becomes an attention target for later tokens (chained shallow steps = deep computation).
Self-consistency (majority vote over N samples) amplifies a STRONG signal but cannot rescue a fundamentally weak model — the binomial makes this concrete.

A Process Reward Model scores EVERY step and exposes the weakest link, giving a richer search/training signal than an Outcome Reward Model, which sees only the final answer and can be fooled by a lucky-but-unsound solution.
MCTS allocates a fixed search budget by looping select -> expand -> simulate -> backprop, concentrating compute on promising subtrees instead of wasting samples on dead ends.

Test-time compute is a SECOND scaling axis: accuracy rises as a saturating power law, PRM-guided search scales steeper and longer than ORM before saturating, and this axis COMPOSES with train-time scale.

existing.name: reasoning-search-tree
existing.issue: Two problems. (1) Dark-mode bug: the central 'beam width / branching factor' caption is dark text on the dark background and is nearly illegible. (2) Weak metaphor: the figure is really three disconnected rows of boxes joined by one ambiguous brace — there are no parent-child edges, nothing shows that Step B nodes descend from a specific Step A node, and no single path is highlighted from Question down to CORRECT. It therefore fails to convey the chapter's opening intuition that 'the answer is the END of a PATH through a tree, not the top of a distribution.' The WRONG/CORRECT leaves float unattached to any branch.
existing.improvement: Redraw as a genuine branching search tree. Root = 'Question' at top. Root branches to 3 candidate first steps (edges drawn explicitly). Each promising first step branches again to 2-3 second steps; let branching be uneven so it reads as real search, not a grid. Terminate several leaves as small 'x' dead-ends (dim) and exactly one leaf as CORRECT. HIGHLIGHT the single root->...->CORRECT path with a bold accent stroke and label it 'the solution is a path'. Dim the un-taken branches to show 'search space explored but pruned'. Put a small side caption: 'a single forward pass returns the top-of-distribution token; reasoning must find the path.' Fix theme-safety: every label must use a theme-aware text color (never a fixed dark hex), and verify the path/caption legibility in BOTH light and dark. Keep node text short (Step 1a, Step 2b, ...); this is a schematic tree, not real content.

new_figures.name: cot-depth-unrolling
new_figures.concept: CoT externalises intermediate state into the token stream, converting a fixed-depth forward pass into a variable-depth sequential computation.
new_figures.rationale: This is the mechanistic heart of the entire first half of the chapter ('Why It Works'), and it is currently only prose. The circuit-depth analogy — a bounded-depth network vs an unbounded sequential program — is exactly the kind of idea a reader must SEE to internalise. It also grounds why test-time tokens buy real computation, motivating everything downstream.
new_figures.placement: Chain-of-Thought Prompting -> Why It Works (right after the 'bounded-depth circuit vs unbounded sequential program' paragraph)
new_figures.brief:
A two-panel side-by-side contrast on a shared theme-aware canvas.

LEFT panel, titled 'Single forward pass = FIXED depth'. Draw a vertical stack of ~4 identical transformer-layer boxes (label them L1..L4). Input tokens enter at the bottom, one answer token pops out the top. Annotate on the side: 'computation depth is capped at the number of layers L'. Below it a caption: 'a hard problem needing more sequential steps than L layers cannot be solved, and the model cannot look back to fix an early error.' Keep it visibly SHORT/shallow.

RIGHT panel, titled 'Chain-of-thought = VARIABLE depth'. Show the model emitting intermediate reasoning tokens into a growing token stream: a horizontal row s1 -> s2 -> s3 -> answer. For each emitted step, draw a curved feedback arrow looping the emitted token back into the context that produces the NEXT step (externalised state). Critically, draw 2-3 thin 'attention' arrows from earlier emitted steps (s1, s2) forward into the computation of a later step (s3), labelled 'earlier steps become attention targets'. Annotate: 'each step is a shallow computation; chaining N steps reaches effective depth ~ N x L, governed by TRACE LENGTH, not layer count.'

Make the visual punch the depth contrast: left is short, right is long/unrolled. Use small illustrative integers only (L1..L4, s1..s3). Theme-safe: layer boxes and arrows use CSS variables; one accent color for the feedback/attention arrows. Everything inside the viewBox, clear left-to-right / bottom-to-top reading order, no overlaps.

new_figures.name: orm-vs-prm-scoring
new_figures.concept: An Outcome Reward Model scores only the final answer; a Process Reward Model scores every step and surfaces the weakest link.
new_figures.rationale: ORM vs PRM is the most heavily emphasised distinction in the chapter (its own section, the interview corner, the reward-hacking warning, and the scaling-law claim 'PRM unlocks steeper scaling' all hinge on it). Readers must intuitively feel that ONE score at the end is blind to WHERE reasoning failed, while a per-step score catches a bad early step even when the final answer looks right. No figure currently serves this.
new_figures.placement: Process Reward Models vs. Outcome Reward Models (anchor after the PRM subsection intro, before PRM-Guided Best-of-N)
new_figures.brief:
One reasoning trace drawn once as a horizontal chain of step boxes: Step 1 -> Step 2 -> Step 3 -> Step 4 -> [Final Answer]. Around this single shared chain, show the two reward models as two contrasting overlays.

TOP overlay = ORM: a single wide bracket spanning the WHOLE trace, collapsing to one arrow into a scalar box on the right labelled 'r_ORM in R  (one number for the entire solution)'. Small caption: 'sees only the outcome — blind to WHERE the reasoning went wrong; a lucky-but-unsound answer can still score high.'

BOTTOM overlay = PRM: a small score gauge/pill UNDER each step showing an illustrative per-step score (use 0-1 or 0-10, e.g. r1 high, r2 clearly LOW, r3 high, r4 high). Flag the low step 2 with a theme-aware warning accent and a small 'weakest link' tag. Then show the aggregation rule to the right: 's = min_t r(step t)' picking up the low r2, with caption: 'a flawed early step dooms the solution even if the final answer happens to be right — PRM catches it, ORM cannot.'

Use only a few small illustrative scores (invent no benchmark numbers, these are schematic 0-1 values). Theme-safe: step boxes and text via CSS variables; one semantic 'warning' accent for the flagged weak step, one 'good' accent for high scores — both theme-aware, not hardcoded primaries. Clear vertical separation between the ORM band and PRM band so the two scoring philosophies read at a glance. Fits fully in viewBox.

new_figures.name: test-time-scaling-curves
new_figures.concept: Accuracy vs test-time compute is a saturating curve; ORM-guided search saturates early while PRM-guided search scales steeper and longer, and the axis composes with train-time scale.
new_figures.rationale: The 'Test-Time Scaling Laws' section is the intellectual payoff of the chapter — the whole reason test-time compute is framed as a new scaling AXIS — yet it is entirely prose plus a worked example. A schematic curve makes four claims click at once: diminishing returns (power law), ORM early saturation / reward hacking, PRM's longer steeper climb, and 'small model + many samples can match big model + greedy'. It ties the reward-model section to the scaling-axis thesis.
new_figures.placement: Test-Time Scaling Laws (after the numbered key findings list)
new_figures.brief:
A schematic line chart. X axis: 'test-time compute (samples N / thinking tokens)' shown log-ish, NO real numbers, just an arrow 'more compute ->'. Y axis: 'accuracy', NO real numbers, just 'higher ->'.

Draw two concave (diminishing-returns) rising curves from a common low-left start:
1) 'Best-of-N with ORM' — rises then FLATTENS early; annotate the plateau: 'verifier can't discriminate among many wrong-but-similar answers; at large N, reward hacking.'
2) 'PRM-guided search' — rises STEEPER and keeps climbing noticeably LONGER before its own gentle flattening; annotate 'PRM unlocks longer, steeper scaling.'
Add a flat dashed baseline near the bottom-left labelled 'greedy CoT / single sample'.

Optionally mark two points to dramatise compute-optimality: a hollow point 'large model, greedy' sitting at some mid height on the y-axis, and a point on the PRM curve at larger N reaching the same height, captioned 'small model + more samples can match a bigger model run greedily.'

Include a small inset (or a compact second mini-panel) titled 'the two axes compose': a 2D field with 'train-time compute ->' on one axis and 'test-time compute ->' on the other, and light contour/shading of accuracy rising toward the top-right corner, labelled 'frontier models push BOTH.' Keep it purely schematic.

Constraints: invent NO benchmark numbers — axes are relative/unlabelled, curve shapes illustrative only. Theme-safe: two distinct theme-aware series colors from the palette for the ORM vs PRM curves, grid/axis via CSS variables. All labels legible in both themes, everything inside the viewBox, curves clearly separated and not crossing ambiguously.

summary: Concept-rich chapter with only two figures, one of which is broken. KEEP mcts-four-phase-loop (clear, legible in both themes). REPLACE reasoning-search-tree: it has a dark-mode illegible caption and, more importantly, a weak metaphor (disconnected box rows, no parent-child edges, no highlighted solution path) that fails to convey the chapter's opening 'reasoning is a path through a tree' intuition — redraw as a genuine branching tree with one highlighted correct path and dimmed pruned branches. Add three foundational figures for core intuitions currently only in prose: (1) cot-depth-unrolling — CoT externalises state, turning fixed-depth into variable-depth computation (the mechanistic heart of 'Why It Works'); (2) orm-vs-prm-scoring — one final score vs per-step scores with the weakest-link rule (the chapter's most emphasised distinction); (3) test-time-scaling-curves — ORM early saturation vs PRM's steeper/longer climb, plus the compose-with-train-time inset (the scaling-axis payoff). All new figures are static because a single frame suffices; briefs enforce theme-safety and no invented benchmark numbers.
